MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Countersigned: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity: §71 KS2026/122 Received Citizen Proposal: Proposal for an Artificial Turf Field from students at Örelund School. Decision: The Municipal Council forwards the citizen proposal to the Municipal Board for processing and decision.

Background: Susanne Öfverberg, together with students at Örelund School, submitted a citizen proposal on 2026-04-29, proposing the installation of an artificial turf field at Örelund School. For some time, the students have expressed a desire for artificial turf on the school grounds, both to create a safer environment and to contribute to greater equality among the schools in the municipality. Now that construction and work are planned for the new plots of land in the area around the school, the students wanted to take the opportunity to submit a citizen proposal – hoping that it could be included in the ongoing planning. Supporting Documents:

  • Citizen proposal regarding an artificial turf field from students at Örelund School (redacted).

Decision: The Municipal Council refers the citizen proposal to the Technical and Service Committee for review and decision.

Background: In her citizen proposal dated 2026-05-11, Lisa Pilqvist suggests the installation of a pedestrian crossing or bicycle crossing on Övregårdsvägen (between the Courthouse and the bicycle path at Björnskogsvägen). Many children and young people cycle along this road on their way to and from school, and currently, there is no pedestrian crossing or bicycle crossing, only a gentle curve in the sidewalk edges. A pedestrian crossing or bicycle crossing would be a simple and effective way to make the route to and from school safer. Supporting Documents:

  • Citizen proposal regarding a pedestrian crossing near the Courthouse (redacted version).

§74 Received Motion: Motion (C) regarding ensuring charging infrastructure throughout the municipality of Bollebygd. Decision: The Municipal Council refers the motion to the Municipal Board for consideration.

Subject: In motion dated 2025-04-25, Yvonne Andrén (C) and Fredrik Olofsson (C) propose that the Municipal Council decide the following:

  • That the Municipal Board be tasked with developing a concrete plan for the expansion of public charging infrastructure in Bollebygd, Olsfors, and Töllsjö.
  • That the plan should identify prioritized locations at park-and-ride facilities, sports facilities, municipal workplaces, and other central destinations, and include collaboration with private companies and network operators.
  • That the plan should ensure that the municipality of Bollebygd meets the EU's requirements for charging infrastructure in both the short and long term.
  • That the possibility of offering charging facilities for employees at municipal workplaces be investigated, in accordance with current tax regulations, as a way to strengthen the municipality's attractiveness as an employer.

Supporting Documents:

  • Motion (C) regarding ensuring charging infrastructure throughout the municipality of Bollebygd.

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Minutes Prepared By: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity: §76 KS2026/103 Adoption of OPF-KR 26 (Transition Support, Pension, and Family Protection for Elected Officials in Municipalities and Regions) Decision: The Municipal Council adopts the regulations regarding transition support, pension, and family protection for elected officials in municipalities and regions (OPF-KR 26), in accordance with the proposal from the Swedish Association of Local Authorities and Regions (SKR).

Background: OPF-KR 26 is an updated set of regulations for transition support, pension, and family protection for elected officials in municipalities and regions, developed by SKR. It replaces previous regulations (OPF-KR 25, OPF-KL 18/14) and is recommended to be in effect from October 15, 2026, coinciding with the new term of office.

A position as an elected official in municipalities and regions is an important part of the democratic structure and requires specific conditions. OPF-KR 26 contains regulations regarding transition support, pension provisions, and family protection for elected officials as defined in Section 1 of Chapter 4 of the Municipal Act. These regulations are adopted locally by the municipal council, regional council, or municipal association and constitute a comprehensive set of rules that clarify the conditions and security for elected officials during and after their term of office. The regulations in OPF-KR 26 are intended to facilitate and enable elected officials to balance their work and their duties, and have been adapted as much as possible to the pension and transition agreements that apply to employees in municipalities and regions. Page 15 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Minutes Verified: [Signature] Official Extract Confirmation: [Organization Name]

The Board of Swedish Municipalities and Regions adopted a proposal for new regulations regarding transition support, pension, and family protection for elected officials (OPF-KR) in December 2025. These regulations are intended to apply to elected officials who assume office after the 2026 election or later. The new version is called OPF-KR 26. The proposal for OPF-KR 26 is based on the premise that a position within municipalities and regions is an important part of the democratic structure and therefore requires special conditions tailored to the position. OPF-KR 26 remains divided into four chapters: introductory provisions, transition support, pension regulations, and family protection. The proposal includes the following changes:

Chapter 2: Provisions regarding transition support for elected officials A new § 2a is introduced, which grants the right to deny transition support if the elected official is sentenced to imprisonment for two years or more. Time spent in a position with another organization that applies OPF-KR 26 or its previous version can be counted towards the qualification period for economic transition support and extended economic transition support. The coordination rules regarding economic transition support and extended economic transition support are changed in § 7. The transition support is proposed to be coordinated with all earned income and/or other income starting from the first year of transition support. For administrative reasons, it is proposed to exempt a certain amount from this coordination, which corresponds to one-twelfth of the annual base amount per month.

Chapter 3: Pension Regulations Amounts less than 250 Swedish krona for a calendar year will not be paid out. In previous versions of OPF-KR, the amount was adjusted annually through indexing, but Page 16 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Reviewed by: [Signature] Confirmation of Extract: This procedure has been removed for administrative reasons. A provision has been introduced allowing for a shorter payout period for pension funds, with a minimum of 10 years. The earliest age for withdrawing pension funds has been adjusted to 62 years. A rule has also been introduced for the automatic payout of pension funds at age 70, to ensure that pension funds are not forgotten. The concept of "cohabitation" is clarified with reference to the Cohabitation Act in § 11, regarding survivor protection. 2025-12-19 3 (4) 20 Chapter 4: Family Protection in the Event of the Death of an Elected Official In § 3, a rule is introduced stating that only one full family protection package can be applied, regardless of whether the individual holds multiple positions with different organizations. In a new note to § 4, the concept of "cohabitation" has been clarified. Comments on OPF-KR 26 The comments on OPF-KR 26 have been revised to clarify and enhance their guidance function. Other An updated actuarial appendix with new distribution factors has been introduced, replacing the previous version from 2014. The appendix has been modernized and describes, among other things, how pension funds should be adjusted for increases in higher ages. SKR (The Swedish Association of Local Authorities and Regions) recommends that local adoption of OPF-KR 26 be implemented with an effective date no earlier than October 15, 2026. OPF-KR 26 is designed for application within each municipality and region. For it to be valid, local adoption of the provisions is required.

Proposal from the Municipal Board to the Municipal Council The Municipal Council adopts the provisions regarding transition support, pension, and family protection for elected officials in municipalities and regions (OPF-KR 26), in accordance with the proposal from the Swedish Association of Local Authorities and Regions (SKR).

§77 KS2025/274 Mobility Plan for the Municipality of Bollebygd Decision: The Municipal Council approves the proposed mobility plan for the Municipality of Bollebygd and instructs the Municipal Board to be responsible for monitoring and overall coordination of the plan.

Background: In December 2022, the Municipal Council tasked the Municipal Board with developing a mobility plan to create a comprehensive approach to more sustainable travel. Throughout 2025, the Municipal Board administration worked on developing a draft mobility plan, which was then sent out for consultation in December 2025. The consultation was sent to all parties in the Municipal Council, committees, as well as the pensioners' council, youth council, associations' council, and sustainability council. Adjustments were made based on the feedback received, as detailed in the consultation report. These adjustments included:

  • The description of responsibilities for each specific action was moved to a separate document.
  • Statements regarding school transportation and railway services were added.
  • The prominent role and importance of cars in mobility were described in more places.

The mobility plan is a strategic document that outlines the direction of the municipality's work on mobility, based on economic, social, and ecological sustainability. It covers walking, cycling, public transportation, and car traffic, and how these... Page 19 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Minutes Verified By: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity: The plan describes the current situation, challenges, and needs within the transportation sector, and outlines key areas for future work. It is based on international goals such as Agenda 2030, national climate and transportation policies, regional goals in Västra Götaland, and the municipality's own governing documents. Supporting documents for the implementation of the plan include appendices detailing responsibilities, descriptions of preconditions, statistical data, and a consultation report. These appendices are not approved at the political level. Particular emphasis is placed on:

  • Increasing the proportion of sustainable travel options.
  • Improving conditions for walking and cycling.
  • Enhancing the attractiveness of public transportation.
  • Developing transport hubs and park-and-ride facilities.
  • Reducing the climate and environmental impact of transportation.

The plan does not entail any direct costs, but will influence future priorities, investments, and operating and maintenance costs. Improved health and a more efficient transportation system will result in socio-economic benefits. The mobility plan provides an important strategic foundation for guiding the municipality towards a more sustainable transportation system. Clear priorities create better conditions for:

  • Effective urban planning.
  • Reduced climate impact.
  • Improved public health.

Agenda Item 78: Program for Social Sustainability in the Municipality of Bollebygd Decision: The Municipal Council approves the Program for Social Sustainability for the Municipality of Bollebygd, dated 2026-03-24.

Background: In the municipal board's operational plan and budget for 2023, the municipal board administration was tasked with developing a plan for social sustainability. This work includes continuing to coordinate and develop strategies to promote integration. Following an external review, a consultation round, and dialogue with the various departments, five areas for development have been identified to enhance the social sustainability of the Municipality of Bollebygd:

  • Conditions for the upbringing of children and young people
  • Living conditions for the elderly
  • Employment and job opportunities
  • Housing and local environment
  • Influence and participation

The purpose of the program for social sustainability is to create a common understanding of the concept of social sustainability and to provide a comprehensive overview of local conditions and challenges. The program also serves as a complement to the municipality's sustainability policy. Furthermore, the program aims to highlight priority areas for the municipality's continued work on social sustainability.

The municipality's business development officer, in collaboration with the municipality's public health strategist, has coordinated and driven this work. The executive management team has been involved in the process. A working group with representatives from the various departments has been formed. Page 22 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Minutes Prepared By: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity:

A working group was established and has contributed the perspectives of the various departments to the work. Further information was gathered from the school's material "Brobygget," where students from Bollebygd's primary schools answered questions about what it's like to be young in Bollebygd.

To ensure the social sustainability program is well-received and that the perspectives of different target groups are considered, the program was sent for review to the municipal committees and expert panels. Below are the adjustments made based on feedback received from each reviewing body:

The Education Committee approved the review comments. Reference number: UN2024/310 §14. The Technology and Services Committee approved the social sustainability program. Reference number: TSN2024/169 §10. The committee suggested improvements. The adjustments made to the program include an update to the list of sources and a shortening of the background section, which previously contained several external references. The Social Services Committee approved the social sustainability program. Reference number: SN2025/82 §7, with comments. The adjustments made include the addition of the following areas to the program: support for individuals with substance abuse and addiction, violence in close relationships, integration of newly arrived immigrants, and the perspective of family relationships. The Building and Environment Committee supports the work on social sustainability and emphasizes its importance in a formal program. The committee has both the responsibility and the opportunity to assist in the work of promoting and preventing issues, which is done through legislation such as the Planning and Building Act and the Environmental Code. Reference number: BMN-2024-1379. The Youth Council approved the social sustainability program on 2024-12-16. Reference number: KS2024/21-7. The Association Council: No comments were received. The Sustainability Council approves the program for social sustainability, taking into account the council's assessment. The comments received and incorporated are related to the formal aspects of the document. A supplementary point regarding the living conditions of children and young people clarifies that the needs for physical and psychological well-being may vary. The goals developed by the departments should ideally be linked to other governing documents and assignments, as well as the Municipal Council's overall goals and budget. The committees are encouraged to utilize relevant expertise within the municipal organization when developing these goals.

The Municipal Council of Seniors (KPR) has responded to the proposal through comments from SPF KS2024/252-12 and PRO KS2024/252-11. The adjustments made to the program include adding the living conditions of seniors as a prioritized area and highlighting the perspective of seniors more clearly throughout the program. The risks associated with involuntary loneliness have also been addressed.

Following an external review, a consultation round, and discussions with the departments, five priority areas have been identified for developing the social sustainability of the municipality of Bollebygd:

  • Living conditions of children and young people
  • Living conditions of seniors
  • Employment and job opportunities
  • Housing and local environment
  • Influence and participation

The committees will develop at least one goal in their operational plans that links to one or more of the priority areas in the program. The committees' goals will be monitored in the committees' annual and interim reports, as well as in the municipality's annual and interim reports. The program for social sustainability will be reviewed annually and revised at least once during each term of office.

The committees' work on developing social sustainability should be included within the committees' annual operational plans and budgets.

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Reviewed by: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity: §79 KS2026/43 Policy on Information Security, Cybersecurity, and Data Protection Decision: The Municipal Council approves the proposal from the Municipal Board. The Municipal Council adopts the proposed policy on information security, cybersecurity, and data protection, dated 2026-04-16. With this decision, the information security policy for the municipality of Bollebygd, adopted on 2022-05-05, § 49, KS2022/63, is repealed.

Background: The information security, cybersecurity, and data protection policy of the municipality of Bollebygd outlines the municipality's overall objectives and principles for protecting information and personal data. The policy covers the municipality's information assets, which include operational information and resources related to information management, such as IT systems, data, employees, and technical infrastructure.

Employees play a central role in information management by receiving, creating, storing, using, sharing, and disposing of information within the organization. All municipal operations handle information about residents, businesses, organizations, and the municipality's own operations. This information must be handled securely and accurately, as it forms the basis of trust and confidence in the municipality.

Page 26 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Countersigned: [Signature] Official Confirmation

This policy is based on the requirements of current legislation, including the new cybersecurity law and the NIS2 directive. After the policy is adopted, it will be further detailed through guidelines for information security, cybersecurity, and data protection, which will be decided by the municipal board. The departments are then responsible for developing procedures that describe how the work should be carried out in each respective area.

The adoption of this policy does not have any direct financial consequences in itself. The work on information security, cybersecurity, and data protection requires the organization to allocate both personnel and financial resources in order to carry out systematic and long-term work. This includes skills development, the creation of procedures, risk assessments, and the development and management of technical solutions. This work is primarily expected to be carried out within existing frameworks, but may eventually require prioritization in the activities' planning.

Information is one of the municipality's most fundamental and valuable assets, just like employees, premises, and technical equipment. The municipality's activities are highly dependent on information being handled correctly and securely. This information is used to make decisions, carry out the activities' mission, and provide services to residents, businesses, and organizations. A large part of the information that the municipality handles concerns individual people and can be both sensitive and, in some cases, crucial to people's safety and security. If such information is lost, altered, or becomes available to unauthorized parties, it can have serious consequences for both the individual and the municipality's operations.

Page 27 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Reviewed by: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity

Information security aims to ensure that information is available when needed, is accurate, and is protected from unauthorized access. Therefore, a systematic approach to information security is a prerequisite for the municipality to operate its activities efficiently and in a legally sound manner, to protect personal integrity, and to maintain the residents' trust in the municipality's operations and services.

Proposal from the Municipal Board to the Municipal Council: The Municipal Council adopts the proposed policy for information security, cybersecurity, and data protection, dated 2026-04-16. With this decision, the information security policy for the municipality of Bollebygd, adopted on 2022-05-05, paragraph 49 (KS2022/63), is no longer in effect.

The Municipal Board's proposal is supported by Ulf Rapp (S).

The regulations for fees have been revised on a couple of occasions, but appendices 1-3 have the same content as when they were initially adopted. Since then, significant changes have occurred in legislation, making it necessary to review and comprehensively revise the fee structure. In addition to assessment and supervision according to the Environmental Code, the fee structure also covers assessment and supervision according to the Act on Animal Feed and Animal By-products, the Act on Precursors to Explosives, and the Radiation Protection Act.

The current fee structure is based on the Swedish Association of Local Authorities and Regions (SKR)'s previous model for a risk-based fee structure. In 2018, SKR presented a new model for a needs-based fee structure. Unlike the previous model, the needs-based model focuses on overall processes and working methods, such as how needs assessments, supervisory planning, and supervisory funding are linked. The model also allows for clear comparability between municipalities that use the same model. The administration therefore proposes a transition to a new fee structure based on SKR's needs-based model. The fee structure consists of four parts:

  • Fee Regulations
  • Appendix 1 = Fees for assessment and registration, as well as certain supervisory activities. Fee levels are used.
  • Appendix 2 = Fixed annual fees (prepayment) for C and H activities.
  • Appendix 3 = Hourly rates (post-payment) for U and UH activities.

Appendix 1 largely corresponds to the existing Appendix 1. The changes mainly consist of the introduction of fee levels and the addition or removal of measures due to changes in legislation or a changed need within the supervisory activities.

Appendix 2 specifies fixed annual fees for environmentally hazardous activities and health protection activities that are subject to registration. Page 30 of 88

Businesses covered by the appendix will pay a fixed annual fee corresponding to a low, normal, or high level of supervisory need. The supervisory time specified in the appendix will be regularly evaluated and adjusted if the need is deemed to have changed.

Appendix 3 lists businesses that do not have a defined assessment level. These businesses pay retroactively based on the actual time spent handling the case. Industries that are not required to register but are deemed to require regular supervision may be moved from Appendix 3 to Appendix 2.

With the proposed transition to a new fee structure, an adjusted hourly rate is also proposed. Apart from index adjustments, the hourly rate for environmental supervision was last adjusted in 2022. Even then, it was noted that the fee was low compared to other municipalities within the Sjuhärad region. Since the fee should reflect the administration's costs for the supervision, it is important to adapt it to current conditions. According to the calculation performed using SKR's template for handling costs per hour, the hourly rate should be increased from 1233 SEK/hour to 1379 SEK/hour. This represents an increase of 11.8%, which is expected to lead to better cost coverage for environmental supervision. The fee structure, along with its appendices, should be reviewed annually and adjusted based on new legislation, changed needs, or other changed circumstances. Since the fee structure should be guided by the need for supervision, the needs assessment plays a more important role in relation to the fee structure and its future development.

The municipal council has approved a proposal to transfer the youth center's organizational structure from the social welfare department to the education department. The education department supports this proposal. The youth council has no objections to the proposal.

Youth Center: The youth center offers open recreational activities for young people from grade 7 until their 18th birthday. The activities are voluntary, drug-free, and free of charge, with a focus on safety, inclusion, and meaningful leisure time. The opening hours have traditionally been Mondays and Wednesdays from 5:00 PM to 9:00 PM, and Fridays from 5:00 PM to 11:00 PM. During periods with fewer visitors, Mondays have been closed, and more outreach work has been conducted in school environments.

Bollebygd, compared to similar municipalities, has a lower range of leisure and cultural activities for children and young people, despite having good staffing and facilities. The social services department has observed that the tasks of the youth development officers have changed over time, with a decrease in tasks related to social services. Furthermore, the tasks have become more complex and require a higher level of expertise, such as social workers and family therapists. To achieve successful outcomes, the methods of work have shifted from an individual focus to a family-oriented approach, which also places higher demands on expertise in social work. The youth development officers' tasks related to social services have also gradually decreased and currently consist of only four cases.

With societal changes, the activities have faced increased complexity in the cases they handle. Several tasks that were previously handled by the youth development officers are now managed through collaborative agreements within the Borås region, for example, through MiniMaria and Barnahus.

Residential support, which was previously part of the youth development officers' responsibilities, has gradually decreased and will be handled by another unit within the social services department (residential support is organizationally part of the functional support and supported housing services starting in November 2025) in order to streamline the responsibilities. The support work in... Page 44 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Reviewed by: [Signature] Certificate of Authenticity:

Individual cases related to young people in supported living arrangements have been discontinued, as the program no longer meets new legal requirements and the need has changed. The youth development workers have always had a close connection to the education department and the students attending municipal schools. Their primary task has been – and continues to be – to operate youth centers and to be present in environments where children and young people spend their time.

Organizational Structure of the Youth Center: The youth center in Bollebygd has had various organizational affiliations over the years. In recent years, the program has been organizationally part of the social services department, which has been appropriate because the youth development workers have had responsibilities closely linked to the work of social services, as described above. Currently, the youth center is organizationally located within the individual and family services resource unit (within the social services department).

Personnel: There are three (3) youth development workers working within the resource unit/at the youth center. Approximately 50% of each youth development worker's position is directly related to the youth center's activities, which corresponds to a total of 1.5 full-time positions. The remaining portion of the positions is used to implement support services determined by the IFO (Individual and Family Services) authority – both for children and young people and for adults – and to conduct outreach work targeting the target group of children and young people, in schools and other environments where young people are present. Currently, the workload distribution for each position is approximately:

  • 50% work directly related to the youth center
  • 25% outreach work, primarily in schools and leisure activities
  • 25% work related to individual cases.

Proposal for a New Organizational Structure: The proposal is to transfer the youth center and two (2) out of three (3) employees from the social welfare board to the education board, specifically to the culture and leisure department. This reorganization will allow the municipality to actively develop leisure activities and, in the long run, offer a more comprehensive range of activities for the municipality's children and young people, focused on leisure. Resources can be used more efficiently within both boards, as the activities will have a more clearly defined role with stronger ties and relevance to the responsibilities of each board. By transferring the youth center to the education board, better management and leadership will be established for the youth workers' primary tasks: operating the youth center and providing supportive and safe environments for the municipality's children and young people. Work at the youth center can be combined with preventative work within schools. This is where the youth workers' skills and training are best utilized. This creates better conditions for preventative work aimed at children and young people directly within the school environment, where the presence of adults creates a safer environment for students. As a result of this change, the social welfare board will retain one (1) of the three (3) employees, allowing them to focus more on the social services' mission: providing a safety net for individuals and families. Cases related to social services will be handled where the appropriate expertise is available, within the social welfare board. In agreement between the education board and the social welfare board, it is proposed that the youth center, including the budget for the building and its operation, along with two employees, be transferred to the education board.

§90 Response to Motion (SD/KD) Regarding Wild Boar Compensation Decision: The Municipal Council approves the motion and instructs the Technology and Service Committee to procure and draft an agreement with a slaughterhouse, and to issue the specified requirements and conditions for compensation necessary for the implementation of a wild boar compensation program.

Background: Daniel Persson (SD), Fredrik Lind (SD), Ewa-Maria Andorff-Ripa (SD), Patrik Karlsson (KD), and Rune Kennborn (KD) propose in a motion received on 2025-11-04 that the municipality of Bollebygd introduce a wild boar compensation program. The motion states that the wild boar population has increased significantly in recent years in much of southwestern Sweden, and this development is increasingly affecting the municipality of Bollebygd. The growing population is causing significant damage to agricultural land, roads, gardens, and other infrastructure. Furthermore, the increased presence of wild boars poses a noticeably greater risk of traffic accidents, especially on country roads where visibility is limited. The problem of wild boars not only has economic consequences for farmers and property owners, but also increases societal costs in the form of repairs, insurance claims, and remediation. At the same time, wild boar meat represents a local and sustainable food resource that is currently not being utilized sufficiently. The proposers suggest that hunters and landowners together receive 1,000 Swedish krona in compensation for each approved wild boar that is killed, but with a maximum of two animals per hunter per week. The landowner and the hunter will agree on how the compensation is to be divided. Page 63 of 88

MEETING MINUTES Municipal Council Meeting Date: 2026-06-04 Minutes Verified: [Signature] Extract Confirmation:

Regarding the proposal to introduce a payment to hunters and landowners who shoot wild boars within the municipality of Bollebygd, the aim is to increase the amount of wild boar meat that is processed for food production.

There is currently no allocated budget for this initiative. The Municipal Council needs to address this as a separate item in 2026, or alternatively, budget funds could be allocated from 2027 to the Technology and Services Committee. Based on the intention of the proposal and an assessment of the external environment, it is reasonable to set a maximum limit of 100 wild boars and 1000 SEK per wild boar shot. This would mean the maximum cost of the payments could be 100,000 SEK per year.

This proposal aligns well with the County Administrative Board's project "Public Food Supply through Local Food," which is a collaboration between the County Administrative Board of Västra Götaland, RISE, LRF Västra Sverige, and Lokalproducerat i Väst AB. The introduction of a payment to hunters and landowners who shoot wild boars within the municipality of Bollebygd aims to increase the amount of wild boar meat that is processed for food production. The payment will be made on the condition that the meat is suitable for use in the municipality's catering services, and it should be seen as part of the effort to reduce the wild boar population within the municipality of Bollebygd. Several municipalities have already introduced some form of wild boar payment, including the municipality of Motala. The municipality has prepared a document with specific requirements and conditions, which is a way to clarify the handling of shot wild boars, the purchase of wild boar meat for the municipality's catering services, and the payment, all of which are in line with the intention of the proposal. There are other alternatives that the Technology and Services Committee could investigate, such as the municipality subsidizing the price per kilogram that hunters receive when delivering wild boars to a slaughterhouse. This would incentivize hunters to both hunt wild boars and deliver them to the contracted slaughterhouse. Page 64 of 88

Agenda Item 92: Party Funding 2026 - Folkets Röst Decision: The Municipal Council resolves to allocate party funding of 61,568 SEK to (FR) for the year 2026.

Background: In December 2020, the Municipal Council established regulations for municipal party funding in the municipality of Bollebygd. These regulations comply with the requirements of the Municipal Act regarding party funding, stipulating that funding is only provided to political parties that are legal entities and hold seats in the Municipal Council. Furthermore, the regulations require each party to submit an annual written report demonstrating that the party funding has been used to strengthen the political party's role in local democracy. This report must cover the period from January 1st to December 31st and be submitted to the Municipal Executive Office within the specified timeframe. The report must include both the party funding received during the year and any funds the party may have saved from previous years. An independent auditor, appointed by the party, must review the report to ensure that the party's use of the funding aligns with the purpose of the municipal party funding. The auditor's report must be attached to the party's report. In a decision made in September 2017, the Municipal Council decided that the allocation of party funding should be made continuously as individual parties submit the required documentation, which Folkets Röst has now done. The party funding consists of a contribution per party and a contribution per seat:

  • 15% of the base amount per seat

tldr: At the municipal council meeting on June 4, 2026, several important issues for the municipality of Bollebygd were discussed, including new plans for transportation, safety, and finances.  The council also received proposals from residents on a wide range of topics, from skate parks to new pedestrian crossings.
lattlast:
The municipality has received several citizen proposals, which are ideas submitted by residents to the politicians. These include proposals to build a skate park in Bollebygd, create an artificial turf field at Örelundskolan, and build a new pedestrian crossing near the courthouse.

Several important plans and regulations will guide how the municipality operates in the future. These include a new plan for transportation within the municipality (mobility plan), a plan for social sustainability, and new regulations for protecting digital information and data. Discussions have also taken place regarding financial regulations for how the municipality should manage its finances.

There are also proposals for changes to the municipality's equipment and buildings. A motion from the Center Party concerns ensuring that there are enough charging stations for electric vehicles throughout the municipality. In addition, a preliminary study has been reviewed regarding the first phase of a new municipal building.

The politicians have also reviewed annual reports, which are documents that show how various organizations and associations performed during the year 2025. These include reports from the fire department and various collaborative organizations in the region. Finally, a decision has been made regarding political party funding, which is the money the municipality provides to political parties to support their work.
